subject: Physics
topic: Classification of Metals, Conductors and Semiconductors
text: Semicoductors: The substances whose conductivity is in between conductors and insulators are called Semiconductors. Solids are also classified on the basis of their Energy bands. According to Bohr model, every electron of an isolated atom has well defined energy level. Inside a crystal each electron will have a different energy level. These different energy levels with continuous variation forms energy bands. The energy band which contains the energy levels of valence electrons is known as valence band. This is the lower energy band. This band is never empty. The upper unfilled energy band is called conduction band. This band is completely empty at 0 K. At room temperature, this band is empty or partially filled with electrons. The separation between the highest energy level of valence band and the lowest energy level of conduction band is known as Energy band gap/Forbiddon energy gap.
text_html:
Semicoductors: The substances whose conductivity is in between conductors and insulators are called Semiconductors.
Solids are also classified on the basis of their Energy bands.
According to Bohr model, every electron of an isolated atom has well defined energy level.
Inside a crystal each electron will have a different energy level. These different energy levels with continuous variation forms energy bands.
The energy band which contains the energy levels of valence electrons is known as valence band. This is the lower energy band. This band is never empty.
The upper unfilled energy band is called conduction band. This band is completely empty at 0 K. At room temperature, this band is empty or partially filled with electrons.
The separation between the highest energy level of valence band and the lowest energy level of conduction band is known as Energy band gap/Forbiddon energy gap.

subject: Physics
topic: Classification of Metals, Conductors and Semiconductors
text: In case of metals, Energy gap is very less or zero or valence band and conduction bands are overlapping. Fig. (a) i and ii. In case of insulators Energy gap is very large. Eg > 3eV Fig. (b) In case of semiconductors Energy gap is small. Eg < 3eV Fig. (c). therefore at 0 K semiconductor behaves as an insulator and at room temperature some electrons in the valence band get thermal energy and jump over to conduction band, hence acquire small conductivity. Energy gap for germanium is 0.72 eV and for Silicon it is 1.1 eV.
text_html:
In case of metals, Energy gap is very less or zero or valence band and conduction bands are overlapping. Fig. (a) i and ii.
In case of insulators Energy gap is very large. Eg > 3eV Fig. (b)
In case of semiconductors Energy gap is small. Eg < 3eV Fig. (c). therefore at 0 K semiconductor behaves as an insulator and at room temperature some electrons in the valence band get thermal energy and jump over to conduction band, hence acquire small conductivity. Energy gap for germanium is 0.72 eV and for Silicon it is 1.1 eV.

subject: Physics
topic: Classification of Metals, Conductors and Semiconductors
text: Classification of Solids by Conductivity Solids are classified as metals, semiconductors, and insulators based on their resistivity (ρ) or conductivity (σ) values. Metals: Elicit low resistivity (high conductivity) ranging from 10^-2 to 10^-8 Ωm (100 to 10^8 Sm^-1) Semiconductors: Display intermediate resistivity (conductivity) between metals and insulators with ρ from 10^-5 to 10^6 Ωm (10^5 to 10^-6 Sm^-1) Insulators: Have high resistivity (low conductivity) with ρ from 10^11 to 10^19 Ωm (10^-11 to 10^-19 Sm^-1) Additional differences will be elaborated in the chapter.
text_html:
Classification of Solids by Conductivity
Solids are classified as metals, semiconductors, and insulators based on their resistivity (ρ) or conductivity (σ) values.

  • Metals: Elicit low resistivity (high conductivity) ranging from 10^-2 to 10^-8 Ωm (100 to 10^8 Sm^-1)
  • Semiconductors: Display intermediate resistivity (conductivity) between metals and insulators with ρ from 10^-5 to 10^6 Ωm (10^5 to 10^-6 Sm^-1)
  • Insulators: Have high resistivity (low conductivity) with ρ from 10^11 to 10^19 Ωm (10^-11 to 10^-19 Sm^-1)

Additional differences will be elaborated in the chapter.

subject: Physics
topic: Classification of Metals, Conductors and Semiconductors
text: Semiconductors of Interest Focus is on inorganic semiconductors, specifically elemental types like Si and Ge. Elemental: Contains materials like Si and Ge. Compound: Includes inorganic (e.g., CdS, GaAs) and organic types. Organic Polymers: Examples are polypyrrole and polyaniline, which are useful in cutting-edge technologies.
text_html:
Semiconductors of Interest
Focus is on inorganic semiconductors, specifically elemental types like Si and Ge.

  • Elemental: Contains materials like Si and Ge.
  • Compound: Includes inorganic (e.g., CdS, GaAs) and organic types.
  • Organic Polymers: Examples are polypyrrole and polyaniline, which are useful in cutting-edge technologies.

subject: Physics
topic: Classification of Metals, Conductors and Semiconductors
text: Energy Bands in Solids Electrons form energy bands rather than discrete levels due to overlapping orbits in solids. Energy bands determine conductivity these include: Valence Band: Contains energy levels of valence electrons. Conduction Band: Located above the valence band, normally empty. Metals have overlapping bands; electrons move freely for conduction.
text_html:
Energy Bands in Solids
Electrons form energy bands rather than discrete levels due to overlapping orbits in solids. Energy bands determine conductivity these include:

  • Valence Band: Contains energy levels of valence electrons.
  • Conduction Band: Located above the valence band, normally empty.

Metals have overlapping bands; electrons move freely for conduction.

subject: Physics
topic: Classification of Metals, Conductors and Semiconductors
text: Energy Band Gap The gap between the conduction and valence bands affects conductivity. Metals: Either overlapping bands or partially filled, low resistance. Insulators: Large energy gap (>3 eV), hindering free electrons. Semiconductors: Small energy gap (<3 eV); limited electrons cross at room temperatures for conduction.
text_html:
Energy Band Gap
The gap between the conduction and valence bands affects conductivity.

  • Metals: Either overlapping bands or partially filled, low resistance.
  • Insulators: Large energy gap (>3 eV), hindering free electrons.
  • Semiconductors: Small energy gap (<3 eV); limited electrons cross at room temperatures for conduction.

subject: Physics
topic: Classification of Metals, Conductors and Semiconductors
text: Bohr Model in Solids A solid's electron energy is influenced by atomic proximity, leading to band formation. Distances affect whether bands split or merge with each other.
text_html:
Bohr Model in Solids
A solid's electron energy is influenced by atomic proximity, leading to band formation. Distances affect whether bands split or merge with each other.

subject: Physics
topic: Classification of Metals, Conductors and Semiconductors
text: Elemental Semiconductor Structure Si and Ge have outer orbits that significantly influence semiconductor behavior. Outer orbit electrons number: Si: Third orbit (n=3) Ge: Fourth orbit (n=4) Their band structures underlay tech advancements in electronics.
text_html:
Elemental Semiconductor Structure
Si and Ge have outer orbits that significantly influence semiconductor behavior. Outer orbit electrons number:
  • Si: Third orbit (n=3)
  • Ge: Fourth orbit (n=4)
Their band structures underlay tech advancements in electronics.

subject: Physics
topic: Classification of Metals, Conductors and Semiconductors
text: Case I: Metallic Conductors Conduction in metals occurs when: Conduction and valence bands overlap enabling electron movement. Valence band partially empty allowing electron mobility within its levels.
text_html:
Case I: Metallic Conductors
Conduction in metals occurs when:
  • Conduction and valence bands overlap enabling electron movement.
  • Valence band partially empty allowing electron mobility within its levels.

subject: Physics
topic: Classification of Metals, Conductors and Semiconductors
text: Case II: Insulators Insulators have no conduction due to: Large energy gap (>3 eV) preventing electron excitation from valence to conduction bands.
text_html:
Case II: Insulators
Insulators have no conduction due to:
  • Large energy gap (>3 eV) preventing electron excitation from valence to conduction bands.

subject: Physics
topic: Classification of Metals, Conductors and Semiconductors
text: Case III: Semiconductors Exhibit limited conduction as small energy gaps allow some electron transition at room temperature. Facilitates limited electron mobility within conduction band.
text_html:
Case III: Semiconductors
Exhibit limited conduction as small energy gaps allow some electron transition at room temperature. Facilitates limited electron mobility within conduction band.

subject: Physics
topic: Classification of Metals, Conductors and Semiconductors
text: Understanding Band Formation When atoms in Si or Ge come together, their outer electrons' energy states form a band structure governed by proximity. Valence band fills at 0 K, conduction band remains empty.
text_html:
Understanding Band Formation
When atoms in Si or Ge come together, their outer electrons' energy states form a band structure governed by proximity. Valence band fills at 0 K, conduction band remains empty.

subject: Physics
text: INTRINSIC SEMICONDUCTORS: Intrinsic Semiconductors are pure semiconductors, in which number of free electrons in conduction band is equal to the number of holes in the valence band. ne = n h = n i Conductivity of Intrinsic Semiconductors is small at room temperature. Electrical conductivity of semiconductor is due to the motion of electrons in the conduction band and motion of holes in the valence band. I = Ie + Ih Hole is considered as a seat of positive charge, having equal charge to that of an electron. Breakage of one covalent bond produces one free electron and one hole.
text_html:
INTRINSIC SEMICONDUCTORS: Intrinsic Semiconductors are pure semiconductors, in which number of free electrons in conduction band is equal to the number of holes in the valence band. ne = nh = ni
Conductivity of Intrinsic Semiconductors is small at room temperature. Electrical conductivity of semiconductor is due to the motion of electrons in the conduction band and motion of holes in the valence band. I = Ie + Ih
Hole is considered as a seat of positive charge, having equal charge to that of an electron. Breakage of one covalent bond produces one free electron and one hole.

subject: Physics
text: Diamond-Like Structures Ge and Si have lattice structures akin to diamonds. These structures are characterized by four nearest neighbors surrounding each atom.
text_html:
Diamond-Like Structures
Ge and Si have lattice structures akin to diamonds. These structures are characterized by four nearest neighbors surrounding each atom.

subject: Physics
text: Valence Electrons Silicon (Si) and Germanium (Ge) each possess four valence electrons. In a crystal lattice, each atom shares these electrons with its four nearest neighbors, forming covalent or valence bonds.
text_html:
Valence Electrons
Silicon (Si) and Germanium (Ge) each possess four valence electrons. In a crystal lattice, each atom shares these electrons with its four nearest neighbors, forming covalent or valence bonds.

subject: Physics
text: Covalent Bonding Model In the crystalline state, Si and Ge atoms' shared electron pairs oscillate between the bonded atoms, which maintains the bond integrity, especially at low temperatures.
text_html:
Covalent Bonding Model
In the crystalline state, Si and Ge atoms' shared electron pairs oscillate between the bonded atoms, which maintains the bond integrity, especially at low temperatures.

subject: Physics
text: Effects of Temperature Rising temperatures provide thermal energy, allowing some electrons to break free and contribute to electrical conduction by creating vacancies (holes) in bonds.
text_html:
Effects of Temperature
Rising temperatures provide thermal energy, allowing some electrons to break free and contribute to electrical conduction by creating vacancies (holes) in bonds.

subject: Physics
text: Holes and Their Charges A vacancy created by a departing electron is called a **hole**, which bears an effective positive charge, acting as a free particle in the lattice.
text_html:
Holes and Their Charges
A vacancy created by a departing electron is called a **hole**, which bears an effective positive charge, acting as a free particle in the lattice.

subject: Physics
text: Intrinsic Semiconductor Characteristics The concentration of free electrons, {tex}n_e{/tex} , is equal to the concentration of holes, {tex}n_h{/tex} in an intrinsic semiconductor, denoted as {tex}n_i{/tex} .
text_html:
Intrinsic Semiconductor Characteristics
The concentration of free electrons, {tex}n_e{/tex}, is equal to the concentration of holes, {tex}n_h{/tex} in an intrinsic semiconductor, denoted as {tex}n_i{/tex}.

subject: Physics
text: Movement of Holes Holes appear to move when electrons jump from neighboring covalent bonds into vacancies. Electrons are not directly involved in this movement, resulting in **hole current** {tex}I_h{/tex} .
text_html:
Movement of Holes
Holes appear to move when electrons jump from neighboring covalent bonds into vacancies. Electrons are not directly involved in this movement, resulting in **hole current** {tex}I_h{/tex}.

subject: Physics
text: Total Current Flow in Semiconductors The total electric current, {tex}I{/tex} , is the sum of the electron current {tex}I_e{/tex} and the hole current {tex}I_h{/tex} as electrons and holes move under an electric field.
text_html:
Total Current Flow in Semiconductors
The total electric current, {tex}I{/tex}, is the sum of the electron current {tex}I_e{/tex} and the hole current {tex}I_h{/tex} as electrons and holes move under an electric field.

subject: Physics
text: Recombination Process Electrons may recombine with holes, maintaining the equilibrium where the rate of charge carrier generation equals the rate of recombination.
text_html:
Recombination Process
Electrons may recombine with holes, maintaining the equilibrium where the rate of charge carrier generation equals the rate of recombination.

subject: Physics
text: Energy-Band Diagram of Intrinsic Semiconductors At absolute zero ( {tex}T=0K{/tex} ), intrinsic semiconductors behave as insulators. At higher temperatures ( {tex}T>0K{/tex} ), thermal energy excites electrons into the conduction band, creating electron-hole pairs.
text_html:
Energy-Band Diagram of Intrinsic Semiconductors
At absolute zero ({tex}T=0K{/tex}), intrinsic semiconductors behave as insulators. At higher temperatures ({tex}T>0K{/tex}), thermal energy excites electrons into the conduction band, creating electron-hole pairs.

subject: Physics
text: EXTRINSIC SEMICONDUCTORS: A doped semiconductor or a semiconductor with suitable impurity atoms is called Extrinsic semiconductor. Doping: Doping is a process of deliberate addition of desirable impurity atoms to a PURE semiconductor. The impurity atoms added are called dopants. Extrinsic Semiconductors are of two types, i) N- type semiconductor ii) P-type semiconductor
text_html:
EXTRINSIC SEMICONDUCTORS: A doped semiconductor or a semiconductor with suitable impurity atoms is called Extrinsic semiconductor.
Doping: Doping is a process of deliberate addition of desirable impurity atoms to a PURE semiconductor. The impurity atoms added are called dopants. Extrinsic Semiconductors are of two types, i) N- type semiconductor ii) P-type semiconductor

subject: Physics
text: ELECTRICAL CONDUCTIVITYOF EXTRINSIC SEMICONDUCTORS Let us consider a semiconductor block of length ‘l’ and area of cross section ‘A’ Let V be the potential difference applied across the ends of the semiconductor. The total current I = I e + I h We know that I = neAv so Ie = n e eAv e and I h = nh e Av h {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = eA(n e v e + n h v h {tex}\frac {I}{A}{/tex} = e(n e v e + n h v h ) {tex}\rho{/tex} = {tex}\frac {RA}{I}{/tex} {tex}\frac {E}{\rho}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {V}{RA} = \frac {I}{A}{/tex} = e(n e v e + n h v h ) {tex}\frac {E}{\rho}{/tex} = = e(n e v e + n h v h ) {tex}\frac {I}{\rho}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {e(n_ev_e + n_hv_h)}{E}{/tex} = {tex}e [n_e(\frac {v_e}{E}) +n_h(\frac {v_h}{E} )]{/tex} {tex}\sigma{/tex} = e ( {tex}{n}_{{e}} {\mu}_{{e}}+{n}_h {\mu}_{{h}}{/tex} ) Where {tex}\mu_e{/tex} is mobility of electron and {tex}\mu_h{/tex} is mobility of hole. Mobility is defined as the drift velocity per unit electric field.
text_html:
ELECTRICAL CONDUCTIVITYOF EXTRINSIC SEMICONDUCTORS
Let us consider a semiconductor block of length ‘l’ and area of cross section ‘A’
Let V be the potential difference applied across the ends of the semiconductor.
The total current I = Ie + Ih
We know that I = neAv
so Ie = neeAve and Ih = nheAvh
{tex}\therefore{/tex} I = eA(neve + nhvh
{tex}\frac {I}{A}{/tex} = e(neve + nhvh)
{tex}\rho{/tex} = {tex}\frac {RA}{I}{/tex}
{tex}\frac {E}{\rho}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {V}{RA} = \frac {I}{A}{/tex} = e(neve + nhvh)
{tex}\frac {E}{\rho}{/tex} =  = e(neve + nhvh)
{tex}\frac {I}{\rho}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {e(n_ev_e + n_hv_h)}{E}{/tex} = {tex}e [n_e(\frac {v_e}{E}) +n_h(\frac {v_h}{E} )]{/tex}
{tex}\sigma{/tex} = e ({tex}{n}_{{e}} {\mu}_{{e}}+{n}_h {\mu}_{{h}}{/tex}) Where {tex}\mu_e{/tex} is mobility of electron and {tex}\mu_h{/tex} is mobility of hole.
Mobility is defined as the drift velocity per unit electric field.

subject: Physics
text: Intrinsic Semiconductor Conductivity The conductivity of intrinsic semiconductors relies heavily on temperature. At room temperature, their conductivity remains low, rendering them ineffective for advanced electronic devices.
text_html:
Intrinsic Semiconductor Conductivity
The conductivity of intrinsic semiconductors relies heavily on temperature. At room temperature, their conductivity remains low, rendering them ineffective for advanced electronic devices.

subject: Physics
text: Enhancing Conductivity with Impurities Immersing semiconductors with impurities, a process known as doping, dramatically enhances conductivity. These improved materials, or extrinsic semiconductors, often use dopants that don't disrupt the original lattice, aligning closely in size with the semiconductor atoms.
text_html:
Enhancing Conductivity with Impurities
Immersing semiconductors with impurities, a process known as doping, dramatically enhances conductivity. These improved materials, or extrinsic semiconductors, often use dopants that don't disrupt the original lattice, aligning closely in size with the semiconductor atoms.

subject: Physics
text: Types of Dopants for Si or Ge - Pentavalent (5 valency) such as Arsenic (As), Antimony (Sb), Phosphorous (P). - Trivalent (3 valency) such as Indium (In), Boron (B), Aluminum (Al). Dopants should align closely in size with Si or Ge atoms to avoid lattice distortion.
text_html:
Types of Dopants for Si or Ge
- Pentavalent (5 valency) such as Arsenic (As), Antimony (Sb), Phosphorous (P).
- Trivalent (3 valency) such as Indium (In), Boron (B), Aluminum (Al).
Dopants should align closely in size with Si or Ge atoms to avoid lattice distortion.

subject: Physics
text: Formation of n-type Semiconductors The inclusion of pentavalent dopants like P in Si creates an extra electron weakly bound in the lattice, thereby increasing conductivity. These electrons, termed donor impurities, become the majority carriers while holes are minority carriers, forming n-type semiconductors.
text_html:
Formation of n-type Semiconductors
The inclusion of pentavalent dopants like P in Si creates an extra electron weakly bound in the lattice, thereby increasing conductivity. These electrons, termed donor impurities, become the majority carriers while holes are minority carriers, forming n-type semiconductors.

subject: Physics
text: p-type Semiconductor Creation Combining trivalent impurities such as B with Si generates a vacancy, or hole, for conduction, consequently forming p-type semiconductors. In these materials, holes are the majority carriers, overshadowing electrons.
text_html:
p-type Semiconductor Creation
Combining trivalent impurities such as B with Si generates a vacancy, or hole, for conduction, consequently forming p-type semiconductors. In these materials, holes are the majority carriers, overshadowing electrons.

subject: Physics
text: Energy Levels in Doped Semiconductors - n-type: Donor energy level is below the conduction band. - p-type: Acceptor energy level is above the valence band. These levels facilitate the movement of electrons and holes with minimal energy input.
text_html:
Energy Levels in Doped Semiconductors
- n-type: Donor energy level is below the conduction band.
- p-type: Acceptor energy level is above the valence band.
These levels facilitate the movement of electrons and holes with minimal energy input.

subject: Physics
text: Carrier Concentrations in Extrinsic Semiconductors In n-type semiconductors, electron density significantly outnumbers holes (n_e >> n_h), while for p-type semiconductors, holes outweigh electrons (n_h >> n_e). Thermal processes recombine minority carriers, making the majority carriers more prevalent.
text_html:
Carrier Concentrations in Extrinsic Semiconductors
In n-type semiconductors, electron density significantly outnumbers holes (n_e >> n_h), while for p-type semiconductors, holes outweigh electrons (n_h >> n_e). Thermal processes recombine minority carriers, making the majority carriers more prevalent.

subject: Physics
text: Band Structure Changes in Extrinsic Semiconductors Doping shifts the energy band structures, forming donor ( {tex}E_D{/tex} ) and acceptor ( {tex}E_A{/tex} ) energy states which modify how carriers move within the semiconductor.
text_html:
Band Structure Changes in Extrinsic Semiconductors
Doping shifts the energy band structures, forming donor ({tex}E_D{/tex}) and acceptor ({tex}E_A{/tex}) energy states which modify how carriers move within the semiconductor.

subject: Physics
text: Understanding Semiconductors with Energy Gaps Conductivity is influenced by the energy gap between conduction and valence bands. For C (diamond), Si, and Ge, energy gaps are 5.4 eV, 1.1 eV, and 0.7 eV, respectively. Tin (Sn), though a group IV element, acts as a metal due to a 0 eV energy gap.
text_html:
Understanding Semiconductors with Energy Gaps
Conductivity is influenced by the energy gap between conduction and valence bands. For C (diamond), Si, and Ge, energy gaps are 5.4 eV, 1.1 eV, and 0.7 eV, respectively. Tin (Sn), though a group IV element, acts as a metal due to a 0 eV energy gap.

subject: Physics
text: General Semiconductor Carrier Dynamics In semiconductors at thermal equilibrium, carrier concentrations satisfy the equation {tex}n_e n_h = n_i^2{/tex} , dictating how intrinsic and extrinsic properties balance each other. This aids in distinguishing between metals, insulators, and semiconductors.
text_html:
General Semiconductor Carrier Dynamics
In semiconductors at thermal equilibrium, carrier concentrations satisfy the equation {tex}n_e n_h = n_i^2{/tex}, dictating how intrinsic and extrinsic properties balance each other.
This aids in distinguishing between metals, insulators, and semiconductors.

subject: Physics
text: N-TYPE SEMICONDUCTOR When pure Ge or Si semiconductor is doped with pentavalent atoms (Bi, P, As and Sb) an Ntype semiconductor is formed. The 4 of the 5 valence electrons of the impurity atoms will form covalent bonds by sharing the electrons, while 5th electron is loosely bound to the parent atom and is free to move. Therefore the number of electrons becomes more than the number of holes. n e > n h and n e n h = {tex}n_i^2{/tex} In an n-type semiconductor electrons are majority carriers and holes are minority carriers.
text_html:
N-TYPE SEMICONDUCTOR
When pure Ge or Si semiconductor is doped with pentavalent atoms (Bi, P, As and Sb) an Ntype semiconductor is formed.
The 4 of the 5 valence electrons of the impurity atoms will form covalent bonds by sharing the electrons, while 5th electron is loosely bound to the parent atom and is free to move. Therefore the number of electrons becomes more than the number of holes. ne > nh and nenh = {tex}n_i^2{/tex}
In an n-type semiconductor electrons are majority carriers and holes are minority carriers.

subject: Physics
text: P-TYPE SEMICONDUCTOR When pure Ge or Si semiconductor is doped with trivalent atoms (Al, In and B) a P-type semiconductor is formed. The three valence electrons of the impurity atoms will form covalent bonds by sharing electrons but one hole is present as the fourth covalent bond is not formed or left empty. Therefore the number of holes become more than the number of electrons. n h > n e and n e n h = n i 2 In a P-type semiconductor holes are majority carriers and electrons are minority carriers. Energy band diagram of N-type(a) and P-type(b) semiconductors.
text_html:
P-TYPE SEMICONDUCTOR
When pure Ge or Si semiconductor is doped with trivalent atoms (Al, In and B) a P-type semiconductor is formed.
The three valence electrons of the impurity atoms will form covalent bonds by sharing electrons but one hole is present as the fourth covalent bond is not formed or left empty.
Therefore the number of holes become more than the number of electrons.
nh > ne and nenh = ni2
In a P-type semiconductor holes are majority carriers and electrons are minority carriers. Energy band diagram of N-type(a) and P-type(b) semiconductors.

subject: Physics
text: P-N JUNCTION DIODE When a P-type semiconductor is brought into close contact with N-type semiconductor, the resulting arrangement is called PN junction diode.
text_html:
P-N JUNCTION DIODE
When a P-type semiconductor is brought into close contact with N-type semiconductor, the resulting arrangement is called PN junction diode.

subject: Physics
text: FORMATION OF PN JUNCTION Thin slices of N-type and P-type semiconductors, called wafers are kept one on the other and heated to a high temperature. Two important processes occur during the formation of PN junction, diffusion and drift. Due to difference in concentration of charge carriers in the two regions in PN junction, the electrons from N side diffuse through the junction into P side. On the other hand holes from P side diffuse through the junction into N side. This is known as diffusion and the current due to this is diffusion current. This creates + ions on N side and -ions on P side, this region where the ions are present and no free charge carriers are present is called Depletion region. The electric field due to these immobile ions creates a potential which is called potential Barrier.
text_html:
FORMATION OF PN JUNCTION
Thin slices of N-type and P-type semiconductors, called wafers are kept one on the other and heated to a high temperature.
Two important processes occur during the formation of PN junction, diffusion and drift. Due to difference in concentration of charge carriers in the two regions in PN junction, the electrons from N side diffuse through the junction into P side.
On the other hand holes from P side diffuse through the junction into N side.
This is known as diffusion and the current due to this is diffusion current. This creates + ions on N side and -ions on P side, this region where the ions are present and no free charge carriers are present is called Depletion region.
The electric field due to these immobile ions creates a potential which is called potential Barrier.

subject: Physics
text: A PN junction can be biased in two ways: (i) forward biasing (ii) reverse biasing In Forward biasing the positive terminal of the battery is connected to P side and negative terminal of the battery is connected to N side. During this process the width of the depletion region decreases and the potential barrier also decreases. A current flows through the junction, which is known as forward current. The diode conducts.
text_html:
A PN junction can be biased in two ways: (i) forward biasing (ii) reverse biasing
In Forward biasing the positive terminal of the battery is connected to P side and negative terminal of the battery is connected to N side.
During this process the width of the depletion region decreases and the potential barrier also decreases. A current flows through the junction, which is known as forward current. The diode conducts.

subject: Physics
text: In Reverse biasing the positive terminal of the battery is connected to N side and negative terminal of the battery is connected to P side. During this process the width of the depletion region increases and the potential barrier also increases. The resistance to the flow of current is high at the junction. The diode does not conduct.
text_html:
In Reverse biasing the positive terminal of the battery is connected to N side and negative terminal of the battery is connected to P side.
During this process the width of the depletion region increases and the potential barrier also increases. The resistance to the flow of current is high at the junction. The diode does not conduct.

subject: Physics
text: PN JUNCTION DIODE CHARACTERISTICS It is the graphical representation between voltage and current through the junction. When the diode is forward biased the current slowly increases up to a certain voltage then it increases exponentially, this voltage is known as threshold voltage or cut-in voltage (0.2 V for Ge and 0.7 V for Si) The dynamic resistance of the diode when it is conducting is given by, r = {tex}\frac{d V}{d I}{/tex} . When the diode is reverse biased, a very small current almost remains the same known as reverse saturation current. After certain voltage, called break down voltage current suddenly increases as shown in graph.
text_html:
PN JUNCTION DIODE CHARACTERISTICS
It is the graphical representation between voltage and current through the junction.

When the diode is forward biased the current slowly increases up to a certain voltage then it increases exponentially, this voltage is known as threshold voltage or cut-in voltage (0.2 V for Ge and 0.7 V for Si)
The dynamic resistance of the diode when it is conducting is given by, r = {tex}\frac{d V}{d I}{/tex}.
When the diode is reverse biased, a very small current almost remains the same known as reverse saturation current. After certain voltage, called break down voltage current suddenly increases as shown in graph.

subject: Physics
text: Introduction to p-n Junction A p-n junction forms the essential building block for semiconductor devices like diodes and transistors. Understanding its behavior is vital for analyzing other devices.
text_html: Introduction to p-n Junction A p-n junction forms the essential building block for semiconductor devices like diodes and transistors. Understanding its behavior is vital for analyzing other devices.

subject: Physics
text: Formation of p-n Junction A silicon wafer can be split into p-type and n-type by adding a pentavalent impurity, creating a metallurgical junction between them.
text_html: Formation of p-n Junction A silicon wafer can be split into p-type and n-type by adding a pentavalent impurity, creating a metallurgical junction between them.

subject: Physics
text: Diffusion Process In a p-type semiconductor, holes diffuse to the n-side and electrons from the n-type move to the p-side, generating a diffusion current across the junction.
text_html: Diffusion Process In a p-type semiconductor, holes diffuse to the n-side and electrons from the n-type move to the p-side, generating a diffusion current across the junction.

subject: Physics
text: Drift Process The electric field arising from space-charge regions causes electrons on the p-side to migrate to the n-side and holes on the n-side to move to the p-side, known as drift current.
text_html: Drift Process The electric field arising from space-charge regions causes electrons on the p-side to migrate to the n-side and holes on the n-side to move to the p-side, known as drift current.

subject: Physics
text: Depletion Region This is the area void of free charge carriers formed as electrons and holes move, comprising positive and negative space-charge regions on opposite sides.
text_html: Depletion Region This is the area void of free charge carriers formed as electrons and holes move, comprising positive and negative space-charge regions on opposite sides.

subject: Physics
text: Balance of Currents Initially, diffusion current surpasses drift current. As space-charge regions extend, drift current strengthens until both balance, stabilizing the junction.
text_html: Balance of Currents Initially, diffusion current surpasses drift current. As space-charge regions extend, drift current strengthens until both balance, stabilizing the junction.

subject: Physics
text: Equilibrium in p-n Junction When diffusion and drift currents are equal, the p-n junction reaches equilibrium, and no net current flows.
text_html: Equilibrium in p-n Junction When diffusion and drift currents are equal, the p-n junction reaches equilibrium, and no net current flows.

subject: Physics
text: Barrier Potential Formed by potential differences due to lost electrons in n-region and gained electrons in the p-region, preventing further carrier movement.
text_html: Barrier Potential Formed by potential differences due to lost electrons in n-region and gained electrons in the p-region, preventing further carrier movement.

subject: Physics
text: Characteristics Under Equilibrium The n-material turns positive relative to the p-material due to electron loss, causing a barrier potential.
text_html: Characteristics Under Equilibrium The n-material turns positive relative to the p-material due to electron loss, causing a barrier potential.

subject: Physics
text: Importance of Barrier Potential This potential opposes electron flow from n to p-region, essential for device stability.
text_html: Importance of Barrier Potential This potential opposes electron flow from n to p-region, essential for device stability.

subject: Physics
text: Semiconductor Diode A semiconductor diode is a p-n junction with metallic contacts and two terminals for external voltage application. The symbolic representation shows an arrow indicating the conventional current direction under forward bias.
text_html:
Semiconductor Diode
A semiconductor diode is a p-n junction with metallic contacts and two terminals for external voltage application.
The symbolic representation shows an arrow indicating the conventional current direction under forward bias.

subject: Physics
text: Forward Bias Condition Occurs when the p-side of a diode is connected to the positive terminal and n-side to the negative terminal. Reduces the effective barrier height, facilitating carrier movement. Forward current increases as applied voltage increases.
text_html:
Forward Bias Condition
Occurs when the p-side of a diode is connected to the positive terminal and n-side to the negative terminal.
  • Reduces the effective barrier height, facilitating carrier movement.
  • Forward current increases as applied voltage increases.

subject: Physics
text: Carrier Movement under Forward Bias Involves minority carrier injection, where: Electrons from n-side reach p-side. Holes from p-side reach n-side. Gives rise to a current due to diffusion.
text_html:
Carrier Movement under Forward Bias
Involves minority carrier injection, where:
  • Electrons from n-side reach p-side.
  • Holes from p-side reach n-side.
  • Gives rise to a current due to diffusion.

subject: Physics
text: Reverse Bias Condition Occurs when the n-side is positive and the p-side is negative, increasing barrier height. Suppresses electron and hole movement. Supports a small, voltage-independent drift current.
text_html:
Reverse Bias Condition
Occurs when the n-side is positive and the p-side is negative, increasing barrier height.
  • Suppresses electron and hole movement.
  • Supports a small, voltage-independent drift current.

subject: Physics
text: Reverse Bias Breakdown The reverse current significantly increases when reverse bias voltage reaches breakdown voltage. Beyond breakdown voltage, the diode can be destroyed due to overheating.
text_html:
Reverse Bias Breakdown
The reverse current significantly increases when reverse bias voltage reaches breakdown voltage.
  • Beyond breakdown voltage, the diode can be destroyed due to overheating.

subject: Physics
text: V-I Characteristics Study Involves examining current variation as a function of applied voltage. A potentiometer enables voltage change. Milliammeter and micrometer measure forward and reverse bias currents, respectively.
text_html:
V-I Characteristics Study
Involves examining current variation as a function of applied voltage.
  • A potentiometer enables voltage change.
  • Milliammeter and micrometer measure forward and reverse bias currents, respectively.

subject: Physics
text: Voltage Threshold in Forward Bias Diode current increases significantly after a characteristic voltage: Threshold voltage for germanium: ~0.2 V Threshold voltage for silicon: ~0.7 V
text_html:
Voltage Threshold in Forward Bias
Diode current increases significantly after a characteristic voltage:
  • Threshold voltage for germanium: ~0.2 V
  • Threshold voltage for silicon: ~0.7 V

subject: Physics
text: Reverse Saturation Current In reverse bias, the current remains small (~µA) and steady with voltage changes until breakdown. This is termed as the reverse saturation current.
text_html:
Reverse Saturation Current
In reverse bias, the current remains small (~µA) and steady with voltage changes until breakdown.
This is termed as the reverse saturation current.

subject: Physics
text: Diode as Current Flow Restrictor A p-n junction diode primarily permits current flow in one direction (forward). Dynamic resistance in diodes is measured as \\( r_d = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta I} \\).
text_html:
Diode as Current Flow Restrictor
A p-n junction diode primarily permits current flow in one direction (forward).
  • Dynamic resistance in diodes is measured as \\( r_d = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta I} \\).

subject: Physics
text: Importance of Diode in Rectification The diode's differential resistance property enables its use in transforming AC to DC, as explored in subsequent sections.
text_html:
Importance of Diode in Rectification
The diode's differential resistance property enables its use in transforming AC to DC, as explored in subsequent sections.

subject: Physics
topic: Application of Junction Diode As A Rectifier
text: RECTIFIER It is a device used to convert AC into DC. There are two types of rectifier circuits, (i) Half wave rectifier (ii) Full wave rectifier HALF WAVE RECTIFIER Principle: The diode conducts only when it is forward biased. During the positive half cycle the diode is forward biased hence it conducts and the output is present across the load resistance. During the negative half cycle the diode is reverse biased so no output. Since the rectified output of this circuit is only for half of the input ac wave it is called as half-wave rectifier.
text_html:
RECTIFIER
It is a device used to convert AC into DC. There are two types of rectifier circuits, (i) Half wave rectifier (ii) Full wave rectifier
HALF WAVE RECTIFIER
Principle: The diode conducts only when it is forward biased.

During the positive half cycle the diode is forward biased hence it conducts and the output is present across the load resistance. During the negative half cycle the diode is reverse biased so no output. Since the rectified output of this circuit is only for half of the input ac wave it is called as half-wave rectifier.

subject: Physics
topic: Application of Junction Diode As A Rectifier
text: FULL WAVE RECTIFIER The circuit uses two diodes and a centre-tap transformer. It gives rectified output for both positive and negative half cycles of AC. Hence it is known as full wave rectifier. During the positive half cycle A is positive, diode D 1 is forward biased and conducts but terminal B is negative, D 2 is reverse biased, and does not conducts, the output is present across the load resistance due to the diode D 1 . During the negative half cycle A is negative, diode D 1 is reverse biased and not conducting but terminal B is positive, D 2 is forward biased, and conducts, the output is present across the load resistance due to the diode D2. So we get output for both half cycles.
text_html:
FULL WAVE RECTIFIER
The circuit uses two diodes and a centre-tap transformer. It gives rectified output for both positive and negative half cycles of AC. Hence it is known as full wave rectifier. During the positive half cycle A is positive, diode D1 is forward biased and conducts but terminal B is negative, D2 is reverse biased, and does not conducts, the output is present across the load resistance due to the diode D1.
During the negative half cycle A is negative, diode D1 is reverse biased and not conducting but terminal B is positive, D2 is forward biased, and conducts, the output is present across the load resistance due to the diode D2. So we get output for both half cycles.

subject: Physics
topic: Application of Junction Diode As A Rectifier
text: Junction Diode and Current Flow The V-I characteristic of a junction diode shows it allows current to pass only when forward biased. Hence, alternating voltage applied causes current flow only during the diode's forward bias phase.
text_html:
Junction Diode and Current Flow
The V-I characteristic of a junction diode shows it allows current to pass only when forward biased. Hence, alternating voltage applied causes current flow only during the diode's forward bias phase.

subject: Physics
topic: Application of Junction Diode As A Rectifier
text: Rectifier and Its Purpose A rectifier uses the diode's unidirectional current flow property to convert alternating voltage (AC) to direct current (DC). A diode conducts current only when forward biased.
text_html:
Rectifier and Its Purpose
A rectifier uses the diode's unidirectional current flow property to convert alternating voltage (AC) to direct current (DC). A diode conducts current only when forward biased.

subject: Physics
topic: Application of Junction Diode As A Rectifier
text: Half-Wave Rectifier Circuit Consists of a diode in series with a load. Pulsating output voltage appears across the load only during positive half-cycles of AC input. The diode conducts in positive half-cycle and is non-conducting in the negative half-cycle.
text_html:
Half-Wave Rectifier Circuit
  • Consists of a diode in series with a load.
  • Pulsating output voltage appears across the load only during positive half-cycles of AC input.
  • The diode conducts in positive half-cycle and is non-conducting in the negative half-cycle.

subject: Physics
topic: Application of Junction Diode As A Rectifier
text: Reverse Breakdown Voltage The reverse breakdown voltage of a diode should exceed the transformer's peak AC voltage to prevent diode damage during the reverse bias phase.
text_html:
Reverse Breakdown Voltage
The reverse breakdown voltage of a diode should exceed the transformer's peak AC voltage to prevent diode damage during the reverse bias phase.

subject: Physics
topic: Application of Junction Diode As A Rectifier
text: Full-Wave Rectifier Circuit Uses two diodes connecting the p-sides to the ends of a transformer's secondary. Each diode rectifies alternate cycles resulting in a full-wave output. More efficient than half-wave rectification.
text_html:
Full-Wave Rectifier Circuit
  • Uses two diodes connecting the p-sides to the ends of a transformer's secondary.
  • Each diode rectifies alternate cycles resulting in a full-wave output.
  • More efficient than half-wave rectification.

subject: Physics
topic: Application of Junction Diode As A Rectifier
text: Centre-Tap Transformer A full-wave rectifier uses a centre-tap transformer, allowing output from both positive and negative AC half-cycles, resulting in a full-wave rectified output.
text_html:
Centre-Tap Transformer
A full-wave rectifier uses a centre-tap transformer, allowing output from both positive and negative AC half-cycles, resulting in a full-wave rectified output.

subject: Physics
topic: Application of Junction Diode As A Rectifier
text: Rectified Voltage Characteristics Rectified output voltage forms pulses of sine half-cycles. Though unidirectional, it isn't steady without filtering.
text_html:
Rectified Voltage Characteristics
Rectified output voltage forms pulses of sine half-cycles. Though unidirectional, it isn't steady without filtering.

subject: Physics
topic: Application of Junction Diode As A Rectifier
text: Role of Filters in Rectifiers Filters, like capacitors and inductors, smooth out pulsating rectified voltage. Capacitors connected parallel to loads stabilize voltage output. Inductor used in series with a load reduces ripple, stabilizing output.
text_html:
Role of Filters in Rectifiers
  • Filters, like capacitors and inductors, smooth out pulsating rectified voltage.
  • Capacitors connected parallel to loads stabilize voltage output.
  • Inductor used in series with a load reduces ripple, stabilizing output.

subject: Physics
topic: Application of Junction Diode As A Rectifier
text: Capacitor as a Filter Charges during rising voltage, discharges during falling voltage through load. Increases time constant, smoothing voltage fluctuation. Output approaches rectified peak voltage.
text_html:
Capacitor as a Filter
  • Charges during rising voltage, discharges during falling voltage through load.
  • Increases time constant, smoothing voltage fluctuation.
  • Output approaches rectified peak voltage.

subject: Physics
topic: Application of Junction Diode As A Rectifier
text: Power Supply with Capacitor Filter Capacitor input filters are common in power supplies. Large capacitors are preferred to improve time constant and output voltage consistency.
text_html:
Power Supply with Capacitor Filter
Capacitor input filters are common in power supplies. Large capacitors are preferred to improve time constant and output voltage consistency.

subject: Physics
text: Introduction to Electron Flow Devices Devices controlling electron flow are essential in electronic circuits and were initially vacuum tubes before transistors.
text_html:
Introduction to Electron Flow Devices

Devices controlling electron flow are essential in electronic circuits and were initially vacuum tubes before transistors.

subject: Physics
text: Understanding Vacuum Tubes Vacuum tubes, also known as valves, control electron flow using electrodes like diodes, triodes, tetrodes, and pentodes.
text_html:
Understanding Vacuum Tubes

Vacuum tubes, also known as valves, control electron flow using electrodes like diodes, triodes, tetrodes, and pentodes.

subject: Physics
text: Function of Vacuum Tubes Electron flow controlled by voltage variation between electrodes. Require vacuum to prevent energy loss through collision with air molecules.
text_html:
Function of Vacuum Tubes
  • Electron flow controlled by voltage variation between electrodes.
  • Require vacuum to prevent energy loss through collision with air molecules.

subject: Physics
text: Characteristics of Vacuum Tubes Bulky and require high power. Operate generally at high voltages (~100 V). Limited lifespan and reliability.
text_html:
Characteristics of Vacuum Tubes
  • Bulky and require high power.
  • Operate generally at high voltages (~100 V).
  • Limited lifespan and reliability.

subject: Physics
text: Introduction to Solid State Electronics Developed in the 1930s, solid-state semiconductors control both the number and direction of charge carriers.
text_html:
Introduction to Solid State Electronics

Developed in the 1930s, solid-state semiconductors control both the number and direction of charge carriers.

subject: Physics
text: Advantages of Semiconductors Over Vacuum Tubes No external heating or vacuum space required. Compact size, low power consumption, low operational voltages. Long life and higher reliability compared to vacuum tubes.
text_html:
Advantages of Semiconductors Over Vacuum Tubes
  • No external heating or vacuum space required.
  • Compact size, low power consumption, low operational voltages.
  • Long life and higher reliability compared to vacuum tubes.

subject: Physics
text: Transition from CRTs to LCDs Mention of the replacement of Cathode Ray Tubes in televisions with more modern Liquid Crystal Display monitors.
text_html:
Transition from CRTs to LCDs

Mention of the replacement of Cathode Ray Tubes in televisions with more modern Liquid Crystal Display monitors.

subject: Physics
text: The Galena Crystal Detector Early use of natural galena crystals with metal contacts as radio wave detectors before formal semiconductor development.
text_html:
The Galena Crystal Detector

Early use of natural galena crystals with metal contacts as radio wave detectors before formal semiconductor development.

subject: Physics
text: Introduction to Semiconductor Physics Exploration of basic semiconductor concepts and the mechanisms behind controlling charge carriers within the solid.
text_html:
Introduction to Semiconductor Physics

Exploration of basic semiconductor concepts and the mechanisms behind controlling charge carriers within the solid.

subject: Physics
text: Overview of Semiconductor Devices Introduction to junction diodes and bipolar junction transistors. Discussion on their applications within electronic circuits.
text_html:
Overview of Semiconductor Devices
  • Introduction to junction diodes and bipolar junction transistors.
  • Discussion on their applications within electronic circuits.
